title: SEGMENT 5 — Allogeneic versus autologous: two different procedures, same name

lines.text: SEGMENT 5 — Allogeneic versus autologous: two different procedures, same name

I've been using two words, sometimes interchangeably, and now I want to draw a sharp distinction between them, because they describe genuinely different procedures with different purposes, different risks, and different patient populations.
The two words are allogeneic and autologous.
Allogeneic means "from someone else.
" An allogeneic HSCT uses bone marrow or stem cells from a separate donor — a sibling, a family member, a registry donor, or a cord blood unit.
The donor's cells are HLA-matched to the recipient as closely as possible, but they are genetically a different person's cells.
The recipient's blood and immune system, after transplant, are essentially the donor's.
If you sequenced a successful allogeneic transplant recipient's white blood cells a year after transplant, they'd come up as the donor's DNA.
The recipient is, in a very real biological sense, a chimera — two genomes living in one body, with one in the blood and the other everywhere else.
Autologous means "from yourself.
" An autologous HSCT uses the patient's own stem cells, harvested before high-dose treatment, frozen, and then re-infused after the treatment has destroyed the marrow.
The patient is, in this case, both donor and recipient.
There is no HLA matching problem because the cells are perfectly matched to themselves.
There is no graft-versus-host disease because there's no foreign donor immune system.
And — crucially for the gene therapy era — there is the option to engineer the cells in the lab between harvest and re-infusion.
The cells go out as one genome and come back as a slightly edited version of the same genome.
These two flavors of transplant exist for genuinely different clinical purposes, and I want you to understand the logic.
Why do allogeneic transplant?
Two reasons.
First, the patient's own marrow is diseased — for example, in leukemia, the marrow contains the cancer; you can't use the patient's own cells because they're cancerous.
So you need a separate, healthy donor.
Second — and this is the subtle one — the donor's immune system, once engrafted, can attack any residual cancer cells in the recipient.
This is the graft-versus-tumor effect, which we'll cover in detail later, and it is one of the most powerful therapeutic forces in oncology.
The donor's T cells see the recipient's leukemic cells as foreign and kill them.
This effect is, in a real sense, an early version of immunotherapy that long predates checkpoint inhibitors or CAR-T.

The trade-off is that those same donor T cells can also attack the recipient's normal tissues — graft-versus-host disease — and balancing those two effects is the central clinical art of allogeneic transplant.
Why do autologous transplant?
Also two reasons.
First, in certain cancers — multiple myeloma, lymphoma — the standard of care is to give a dose of chemotherapy so high it would normally be lethal to the bone marrow.
The autologous transplant is a rescue — you collect the patient's stem cells beforehand, give the high-dose chemo, and re-infuse the cells to rebuild the marrow.
You're not using the transplant to replace diseased cells; you're using it as a safety net that lets you give an otherwise impossible dose of chemo.
Second — and this is the modern reason that's exploded over the last decade — autologous HSCT is the chassis for gene therapy.

You take the patient's own HSCs, edit them in the lab to fix a genetic disease — Casgevy edits the BCL11A enhancer to reactivate fetal hemoglobin in sickle cell, Zynteglo adds a functional beta-globin gene for beta-thalassemia — and re-infuse the edited cells.
The autologous step is the delivery mechanism.
The gene editing is the therapy.
So when you read about HSCT or gene therapy, the first question to ask is always: is this allogeneic or autologous?
Because the entire risk profile flows from the answer.
Allogeneic risks: graft failure, graft-versus-host disease (acute and chronic), opportunistic infections (especially CMV, EBV reactivation), all the complications of immunosuppression.
Treatment-related mortality at one year for allogeneic transplant is, depending on patient age and conditioning intensity, somewhere between 10 and 30 percent.
It is a major procedure with real death risk.
Autologous risks: the conditioning chemotherapy toxicity (mucositis, infections during the cytopenia period), but no GVHD.
Treatment-related mortality at one year is typically 1 to 3 percent.
Much safer.
But autologous can't, by itself, treat cancers where the bone marrow itself is diseased, because you'd be collecting contaminated cells.
So allogeneic remains the standard for most acute leukemias, while autologous is standard for myeloma, certain lymphomas, and the gene therapy indications.
There's a third category I'll mention here and we'll come back to: syngeneic transplant, which means donor and recipient are identical twins.
Same genome, perfect HLA match, no GVHD risk and no rejection risk.
Clinically it's like autologous — no immune incompatibility — but the donor cells are guaranteed not to carry the original disease (because they came from a separate body).
In the rare cases where a leukemia patient has an identical twin, this is essentially the ideal transplant scenario.
But identical twins are rare, so syngeneic is a tiny fraction of the field.
I mention it mostly to complete the taxonomy.
So the taxonomy: allogeneic, from a different person; autologous, from yourself; syngeneic, from an identical twin.
Three flavors.
Allogeneic for blood cancers where you need the graft-versus-tumor effect and a clean marrow source.
Autologous for chemo rescue and for gene therapy.
Syngeneic for the rare lucky patient.
That's the architecture of the field.
Let's now go through the actual diseases this procedure treats.
